moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Polish Opening, an unusual first move that immediately claims space on the queenside and prepares to fianchetto our bishop on b2. While 1.b4 looks offbeat, it's a legitimate choice that avoids mainstream theory—you're not fighting the center directly, but instead controlling important squares like c5 and a4. This move works especially well if your opponent plays passively, as you can build pressure along the long diagonal and on the c-file.

Black develops naturally toward the center and prepares to support the critical d5 and e4 squares. By playing Nf6, Black is flexible—your opponent can head toward a kingside fianchetto (as they eventually do) or pivot toward a standard Indian setup. This is a sound developing choice that doesn't commit Black to any particular plan yet.

We complete our fianchetto setup, placing the bishop on the long diagonal (a1-h8) where it puts pressure on e5 and eyes the center. The bishop on b2 is a key piece in the Polish Opening strategy, and it works best when paired with control of the d4 and c5 squares. This move also keeps things flexible—we haven't committed to a specific pawn structure yet, so we can adapt based on Black's setup.

Black mirrors your setup with a kingside fianchetto, preparing Bg7 to control the long diagonal and creating a solid, flexible position. By playing g6, Black adopts a King's Indian-style setup that's fundamentally sound and gives your opponent good piece placement. Now the position is locked into a quiet battleground where both sides have fianchettoed bishops pointing toward the center.
